sentences.text: For my good luck, we were always together.
I don't think that, without Annetta, I would have survived, not only because we were twins and we were selected to live because we are twins, but we were encouraging each other.
We were helping each other.
We were-- I remember later on Annetta had typhoid.
And only because we were together, somehow she recovered.
I really don't think I would have been able to get through everything without her.
Oh, well, we had been given rations.
It was, in the morning, some kind of liquid, which was called coffee or tea.
But it was only lukewarm sometimes.
Maybe sometimes it was warm.
It was carried in a big-- big containers like you use for-- I don't know what-- big wooden container with some handles.
And four girls had to carry it.
And that was a privilege to carry it because you could take a little bit out for yourself if it was soup or something.
I know that one day I had been able to help.
And I was able to get a little bit more of soup.
We get-- were given that soup.
We were given a portion of bread.
I don't know how much was it, but it was very little.
And little somebody ate it straight away not to lose it or because they were hungry and they felt like filling themselves up and then they didn't have anything else.
Some people were hiding their bread.
But there were occasions when it was lost because somebody else has eaten it, you know.
It was very difficult to keep something just for your own.
That was our ration.
In the sick bay, we might have got extra ration of something what was given to the patients and that they didn't eat because they were sick or they might have died.
So if somebody died, his portion was always divided between the others.
We had always just the same.
When we came, we had been stripped of our clothing.
You have to realize that we have been having our suitcases-- they were not-- never given to us.
They were just taken away.
They were kept there.
And there was special treatment of all the things that prisoners brought to Auschwitz.
Good stuff was separated, and it was sent to Germany.
And other stuff was given to the prisoners as clothing, some of it, not good-- not good stuff.
We have been given a dress.
It didn't matter if it fitted or not.
Sometimes a big girl got small dress, then there was exchange.
We got some-- we had our shoes.
We had good shoes because I have been given-- I must tell you.
I have been given once shoes, ski shoes, when I have been carrying a-- a dead body-- corpse-- to the crematoria.
That was not crematoria.
It was the laboratory of Mengele.
And that was-- I'm not sure where.
I have been given by one of the kapos, one of the prisoners who was in charge of clothing, secretly he gave me a pair of shoes.
And I carried it back into the camp, in our camp, under the blanket where the corpse was before.
So I had shoes.
But we have been given only clothing there what we were using all the time.
If we were able to wash it in cold water and dry it, that was fine.
But we haven't been given any exchange, any change of clothing.
Twins and some doctors.
But not living in the same barrack.
In the same barrack where we were overnight, there were other people from-- other girls from Czechoslovakia who came with us from Theresienstadt at the beginning, in the family camp.
Yes, later on, we had other nationalities there.
There were Polish-- there were-- there were from Holland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't not know if there were French in ghetto-- no, in-- in Auschwitz.
I don't know.
Polish were there.
We didn't hear anything.
We didn't know anything.
We just knew that we were selected for a special, special kind of work.
We didn't know it would be experiments on human beings.
We had been taken to laboratory of Mengele, which was not at the same camp where we were.
And he was measuring us.
He had an interpreter.
He had some girl there who was taking sketches.
Yes.
Her name was Dina Gottlieb.
And she is survivor.
She lives in America.
And she was doing sketches.
And there was-- there was x-rays.
There were gynecological examinations and injections.
I really can't remember what-- what tests did they make.
They wanted to know if we are identical.

And once they established that we are identical, Annetta and I, we were supposed to be-- we didn't know that then, but we were told about it later-- we were supposed to be mating with twins, identical twins, prisoners of course.
And we were supposed to become pregnant.
Because Mengele wanted to know if identical twins have children with identical twins if their offspring will be twins again, if it's in the genes.
I don't know how much scientific-- scientific knowledge did he have, but he was trying.
And how much sense was in these tests, I can't say.
It seems to me that it was pointless what he was doing in many occasions.
But that was the test he was doing.
And we have been taken to one of the-- to-- to the laboratory.
I don't know exactly where it was.
There were two beds.
On one bed was one of the twins, the male.
On one bed was one of us.
And then, in just a very primitive way, we were given transfusion of blood of the boys.
Separately-- one of us and one of them, and then later, the next one.
It was painful because it was big amount of blood.
And it was big needle.
And it was not-- not much hygienic there.
And I don't think that we have been given some-- some Novocaine or something.
It was painful.
And it was frightening because our blood, we were given blood by somebody.
We were having a very bad reaction because it was not compatible blood.
I don't know why.
I don't know if, 50 years ago, they didn't know how to diagnose which blood group or so.
We had very bad reaction.
We were sick, but we didn't die.
And it might have been-- I don't know if it was the reason that Mengele didn't continue straight away with the test-- with the experiment.
Maybe he wanted to get for us different pair of twins, I don't know.
Maybe because of the blood transfusion, we haven't been put to the test, to the experiment straight away.
No, no, we couldn't.
They spoke Polish, and we speak Czech.
And we were not allowed to talk, really.
We maybe said something like hello from where you are, but we were not allowed to talk to each other.
Oh, yes, we were together there.
We were talking together.
But we didn't really get any news from them about their experiments.
We somehow-- we didn't know anything.
We were really isolated.
Now, after this transfusion, what happened to the twins, I don't know.
We have never seen them again because they were male, and they were in different camp.
Annetta and I were sent back to the block where we were.
And the experiment didn't continue at that time.
We recovered from the transfusion.
And the-- we were told that we would have to copulate with those boys, and we would have to become pregnant.
Eventually, we would be pregnant.
And then they would have to killed us to find out what we carry.
They wouldn't have kept our children.
They wouldn't have kept us.
They didn't want us or the children.
They just wanted the guinea pigs.
I mean, Mengele's aim was to find out how to make the German population faster, bigger.
But it was fortunate for us.
It was at the time that the Allies came near and Mengele had to pick up his business.
He couldn't continue with the experiments.
I don't know what army came nearer to Auschwitz.
We were liquidated.
That camp where we were-- Birkenau, B2B, was liquidated.
We were sent to Frauenlager, women camp, for a short time.
And from there we were sent on a death march to elsewhere, to Ravensbruck.
We were only told that we were supposed to be doing this and that by somebody who was to know, one of the helpers.
We didn't know.
Mengele wouldn't have told us.
Yes.
That is one thing that I would like to you.
When we were experiment-- experiment guinea pigs, Mengele took my sister and myself specially to see the gypsy barracks.
And we were amazed that the children, women, and men were together.
They looked quite gay-- happy.
They have looked healthy.
They probably were not there long.
And they were together.
That was a very important thing for us that we saw that they are better off than us.
And we didn't know the reason.
We just knew that they have musical instruments there.
And Mengele, who was a lover of music, cultured man, made them to play music for us.
And when we came out of there, we thought, they are much better off.
But we found out the next day that they were sent to gas chamber the day after.
Why did he take us there?
I wouldn't know.
Maybe he wanted to show us that if we cooperate with his experiments, that he-- we would be better off.
I don't know.
He didn't talk about anything to us.
I had one occasion when I talked to him, I took the courage-- I don't know what possessed me.
I was told that my husband will be sent to work somewhere out of-- in [? other ?] camp to go to east.
And through interpreter, we were not allowed to talk just like that.
But I asked Mengele if he would let him stay there.
After all, I was married, and I would like to be together.
And I don't want to be in an experiment.
I didn't tell him that I know anything because I was not supposed to.
But I would like to be with my husband.
He should stay in the camp or I could go with him.
And he just-- no.
That is-- you are just a number.
There is nothing that you can say what to do, what should be done, or something.
You know.
Yes, the conversation was finished.
But he was-- he did listen.
He was-- he was there, but for him, it was just nothing, no request.
I have thought a lot about death.
And I saw the death daily around me.
But I somehow didn't think that I would be the one to be in that position of having death on my shoulders.
I always believed that I will come alive out.
I don't know what made me to be so optimistic.
I suppose that it was the fact that Annetta and I were together, that we could say to each other, look, tomorrow maybe it will be better.
But we didn't philosophize.
There was no time to think and to talk about it.

The daily trial of living, to be able to go to toilet, to be able to go to have-- to the washroom in time that was allocated to us, to be able to eat your bread or have the soup, you had to queue for everything.
You had to-- you know, life was dull.
We haven't been bored because we have been always trying to survive every minute of the day.
I didn't talk to him just like that, Hauptsturmfuhrer, Dr.
Hauptsturmfuhrer, I don't know.
I can't remember.
I can't even remember how did I talk to him.
I know I did.
But I can't remember.
I don't-- I remember one thing.
I wasn't frightened to approach him.
I wasn't frightened at that time.
Maybe I was shivering afterwards.
But at that moment, I had the-- the reason to approach him.
Well, he was a person that you could really think that he's nice.
If you wouldn't have met him there, if you wouldn't have known what is he doing, you would have even thought that he's a nice man-- when he was whistling.
We knew that he had a wife and a child in Germany.
And the child was sick-- we were-- on the grapevine, we were told.
And I know that we have wished the child should die, that he would know what sorrow is.
The child didn't die.

I didn't witness his behavior in any other way than just examining other twins, sitting there, measuring, writing, talking to the-- through the interpreter, Polish interpreter, telling them something.
He even gave my sister and me white bread and some cheese.
I think it was after we had the transfusion, I'm not sure.
I'm not sure.
But I haven't seen him doing anything to anybody.
I only know what the witnesses told us at the trial of Mengele in his absence.
In '85, Mengele was tried by the Nuremberg judges-- prosecutors.
The trial-- trial was in Jerusalem.
And Jana Wendt, from took Annetta and myself as survivors-- twins-- from Australia.
We were the only ones they could find here.
And there were other twins who were survivors from all over Europe, all over the world-- Israel, America, and some-- some came from Europe.
Not only twins, but they were also dwarfs.
And there were also doctors who were in charge.
And they were giving testimony about Mengele's students.
And then I have heard about trial-- about the experiment that he has made on others.
For instance, toddlers, twins, that he had then sewn together like Siamese things.
Nowadays, they separate them.
He had them sewn together to see how they exist as Siamese twins.
There were two boys.
One of them sang beautifully.
They were about 15.
The other one didn't.
Mengele wanted know the difference.
I mean, he wanted to know a lot-- scientist.
He was an anthropologist, really.
He had then operated on their vocal cords.
One of them died.
And the other one didn't.
He survived, but he lost his voice.
At the trial, he was standing at the witness stand.
And he was talking with the help of an instrument, like a metallic voice, like a robot.
And he said, 40 years-- this was in '85-- 40 years ago, one Nazi took my voice away.
He took my brother's life away.
He took my voice away.
Now, as a German, made it possible for me to talk to you and tell you about it.
That was one of the things.
There was one woman who was not a twin, but she was also an experimented on by Mengele.
And she has given evidence.
She lives in Israel.
She was one of Czech girls.
She came to Auschwitz and went through selection unnoticed that she was pregnant and was sent to Germany to work.
But when they found out that she was pregnant, she was sent to Auschwitz back.
They thought that she was sent to gas chamber.
But Mengele didn't send her to gas chamber.
He used her for experiments.
He let her carry on with her pregnancy.
She was cared for.
The baby was delivered.
And the baby was lying next to her on the bed.
And her breast was tied.
She was not allowed to feed the child.
The infant-- infant was lying next to her, crying, whimpering.
And she was supervised.
She was not allowed to feed the child with her milk.
She probably could have had milk.
She tried sometimes to put her finger into a bowl of soup that she was given for the baby to suck.
But it didn't help.
The child was suffering.
I don't know how long was it, but one nurse obtained-- or was it a doctor, I'm not sure who; I didn't see her, I know only of it-- obtained some syringe and a drug.
And the mother was given it to inject her own child, kill-- kill it to save it from further suffering.
And the baby, the corpse, was then taken out and thrown out with the other corpses.
And when Mengele came, he wanted to see the baby.
And the baby wasn't there.
He was very angry.
I don't know how did it happen that no-- that woman was not punished.
I don't know what happened then.
The woman survived.
She was sent somewhere else.
But Mengele's experiment was spoiled because he couldn't find out how long unfed baby-- newborn baby-- would live.
That was one of his experiments.
We were just like dogs waiting until he whistles to be taken there and here, to be examined, or do this and that.
You know.
He was the master of our lives.
We knew that he is holding our lives in his hands.
We could have been sent to work somewhere in Germany.
Maybe we would have survived.
We might have been sent to gas chamber.
But we were quite young, so we probably wouldn't have, at that time, when we arrived.
But in a way, because selecting us for that special purpose of his, he might have saved our lives.
We might have been sent to Germany to-- or something to work.
And we might have died of exhaustion, I don't know.
It's difficult to say.
He used us.
He saved us for his purpose.
Maybe he saved our lives.
I don't know.
I wouldn't be grateful to him for that for what he was doing.
He didn't really consider us as human beings.
He considered us as his things for his experiments, his guinea pigs.
For him, we were not really Annetta and Stephanie.
We were just the guinea pigs.
I don't think much about him because I try to forget whatever.
But he was a cruel man who was trying to supply German university.
He was supplying all his experiments.
He was experimenting with eyes.
He was dissecting the corpses probably for different kinds of colors of eyes.
If twins had two different kind-- twins, one twin had different color of eyes, he wanted to know what the reason was.
He probably killed them and had the eyes removed to find out.
I don't know.
I can't say anything of those things which I haven't seen or wasn't witness of.
I have been told about those things.
Nothing, really.
Nothing, really.
We just didn't talk about it.
There was not much to talk about, nothing.
We just didn't discuss it.
We just took it as it came.
Yes, not that I would have been involved.
But I have being once sitting in front of the barrack.
I remember it was a wonderful, sunny day.
There was no grass, no flowers, nothing.
But I was sitting in front of the barrack.
And there came a Polish prisoner who was in charge of something or other.
She had a better position.
She was better looking, she was very nourished, and she was better dressed.
And she sat there with me, and she started to fondle me.
I didn't understand that.
I was really naive at that time.
And that was one of them, I think.
She didn't continue because I probably was not responding enough.
But I know about the other homosexual.
For instance, the lageralteste was a German prisoner who was called Orie-- Orie.
And she was in prison, I think, because she was against the regime or that she was a Communist.
I'm not sure which one.
She was good looking, energetic, small woman, full of energy.
And she has chosen one of our girls-- good-looking, Slovak girl-- and she has been-- she was given good clothes.
And she was always with her.
I don't know if she lived with her or if she just visited her, I don't know.
But we knew that there is the relationship between those two women.
And that was the reason why that girl survived.
Well, I had one occasion when I was given a task to wait for the distribution of the bread.
And the bread didn't come yet.
And there was one of the German prisoners who was in a higher position.
He was a German.
I don't know why was he in concentration camp.
He was in charge of the whole-- whole block, I think.
And he was very pleasant.
And he was talking to me, telling me about his family and giving me to understand that it's not his doing that we are here, that he's there just as well because of his-- I don't know what happened.
He didn't tell me about himself.
He just talked about his family, whom he misses.
And later on, in a different camp, we were looked after by a Wehrmacht.
That was the German army, old-- older man.
In my opinion, he was old.
He was probably just 40.
And he was-- we called him Papi because he never hit anybody.
And he was gentle.
He was kind.
He didn't shout.
And there was-- I was supposed to take the latrines buckets out.
And there was no light.
So I had to tell him.
And we were-- before somebody came and repaired it, he was talking to me.
And also, just like a human being, he took me as a-- you know, somebody to talk to.
He didn't want to hear from me because he knew about us a lot.
But he wanted to talk about himself.
And he said, you know, I'm just like you, a prisoner here, the regime-- prisoner of the regime.
I have my family.
And I don't like to be here.
I would like to go.
I don't know what, was he having a farm or something?
But he was kind.
And those occasions and kind people talked to us-- that was very, very valid.
For us, it was something, you know, there is still a humanitarian bond, and people are human.
And we are not only thought as a, you know, number-- we have been numbers only.
But somebody thinks we are human beings.
It-- we were there a year within the camp.
But the experiments didn't take part-- didn't take part all the time.
And we have been just staying there.
We haven't been really experimented all the time.
But we were one year in Auschwitz.
